IMPACT.justification: Creating the first Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) and releasing a trained parser addresses a clear resource gap for a low-resource, creole-like variety and will materially enable downstream research. The demonstration that English syntactic knowledge transferred via feature-level neural stacking yields large gains (36% relative error reduction; UAS 84.21%, POS 84.79%) provides a compelling, practical method for leveraging high-resource languages to improve dialectal parsing. While the dataset size and some annotation limitations constrain immediate generality, the released data, code, and strong transfer results are likely to be widely used and to open follow-on work on low-resource and non-standard language varieties.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This work is squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue: it introduces a new Universal Dependencies resource (1,200-sentence Singlish treebank), applies and extends state-of-the-art dependency parsing models (Dozat & Manning biaffine parser) and neural stacking for cross-lingual transfer from UD-Eng, and reports standard metrics (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy) with clear gains (36% relative error reduction, best UAS 84.21%). The audience expects resource creation plus empirical transfer techniques, the methodology and evaluations follow community standards, and releasing code/data further increases its relevance to the venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides substantial experimental support for its claims: it constructs a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, trains POS and dependency models (bi-LSTM-CRF and biaffine parser), and demonstrates large gains from feature-level neural stacking versus base Singlish and English baselines (36% relative error reduction, UAS 84.21%, POS 84.79%). Reproducibility is well addressed (code and hyperparameters released), and limitations/failure modes (limited training data, issues with null subjects/copula, NP deletion cases) are explicitly discussed. Minor gaps remain — the summary does not indicate extensive ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., transfer method comparisons or training-data-size sensitivity), so while the experiments are solid they are not exhaustively analyzed.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper makes a notable novel contribution by creating and releasing a new Singlish UD dependency treebank (1,200 sentences), which is a genuine new resource for the community. Methodologically it combines existing strong architectures (biaffine parser, biLSTM-CRF POS tagger) with feature-level neural stacking to transfer English syntactic knowledge to Singlish; neural stacking itself is not new, but its application here for cross-dialect transfer and the demonstrated large gains (36% relative error reduction) constitutes a meaningful, non-trivial extension. The authors clearly differentiate their work from Dozat & Manning (2016) and other baselines and provide code and annotations, making the contribution informative to experts even if it is not a wholly new algorithmic idea.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-written and well-organized: the main contributions are clearly stated early (construction of the Singlish UD treebank, parser training, and neural stacking gains) and the methodology, datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics are presented coherently with hyperparameters and code made available. Notation and definitions seem consistent enough to follow (no issues reported) and related work coverage is adequate. Presentation is effective overall—baselines, metrics (UAS/LAS/POS accuracy), and limitations are reported—so only minor clarity/details (e.g., explicit comments about figure/table efficacy) prevent a top score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s central claims (construction of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and large parsing/POS gains from English knowledge via feature-level neural stacking, e.g., 84.21% UAS and 36% relative error reduction) are stated and supported by concrete experimental results and comparisons to sensible baselines (base Singlish parser, Dozat & Manning model, baseline POS tagger). There are no formal mathematical derivations required and none appear incorrect. Methodologically the experiments are sound overall (use of UD-Eng for transfer, released code and hyperparameters), but the summary does not indicate statistical significance testing and notes comparability issues (no fine-grained POS for UD-Eng) and small Singlish training data — minor weaknesses that do not overturn the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against strong and relevant baselines (Dozat & Manning 2016 biaffine parser, a Singlish-only baseline, and a BiLSTM-CRF POS baseline) and reports standard metrics (UAS/LAS/POS accuracy) on the same Singlish treebank. It also analyzes where neural stacking helps and where it struggles (e.g., NP deletion cases). A minor caveat is that they did not use fine-grained POS tags for UD-Eng which affects comparability to some prior results, but overall the comparisons are fair and the most relevant competing approaches are cited and discussed.

main_claims: Constructed a Singlish dependency treebank of 1,200 sentences under the Universal Dependencies scheme.
Trained a Singlish dependency parser based on the Dozat & Manning (2016) biaffine attention model and improved it by integrating English syntactic knowledge using feature-level neural stacking.

Showed that English knowledge transferred via neural stacking yields large gains (36% relative error reduction), achieving a best UAS of 84.21% for the Singlish parser and improved POS tagging via neural stacking (84.79% accuracy).
Released the Singlish annotation and parser for further research.
_summary.methodology: Created a Singlish UD treebank and trained neural POS and dependency models: a bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger and a biaffine-attention bi-LSTM dependency parser, then applied feature-level neural stacking to inject representations and features from models trained on English (UD-Eng) into the Singlish tagger and parser.
datasets: ICE-SIN (Singapore Component of the International Corpus of English)
English Gigaword (Giga100M sample used to train embeddings)
baselines: Dozat and Manning (2016) biaffine parser (base English parser)
Base Singlish parser trained on Singlish treebank only
Bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger baseline (Chen et al., 2016)
limitations: Limited amount of Singlish training data; Singlish POS tagger trained on treebank alone had unsatisfactory accuracy.
They do not recover deleted copula or deleted noun-phrases (null subjects/objects) in the annotations.
Neural stacking model shows slightly lower (yet competitive) performance on NP Deletion cases.
They did not use fine-grained POS tags for UD-Eng experiments, which affects comparability to some prior results.
